topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a bright, unwelcome intrusion on my otherwise quiet afternoon. It was a post from Mr. Harrison, my AP History teacher. A picture of him, smiling broadly, captioned with a vaguely philosophical quote. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked on it, my fingers hovering over the screen as if expecting it to burn. Seeing his face, so familiar yet suddenly alien in this digital context, made my skin crawl. It felt like being exposed, scrutinized under a harsh, unforgiving light. I quickly minimized the window, my breath catching in my throat, and focused intensely on the textbook open on my desk, trying to anchor myself back to the tangible reality of dates and treaties, anything but this digital trespass.

[story 2]

I scrolled through my feed, a slow, deliberate movement that felt heavy with resentment. There it was: Mr. Harrison’s profile. His recent stories showed glimpses of his personal life—a casual dinner, a weekend hike. The casualness of it was what truly unsettled me. He was supposed to be an authority figure, a guide through the complexities of the past, yet here he was, sharing snapshots of his leisure. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my hands tremble slightly. I slammed my phone face-down onto the wooden surface of my desk, the small impact sounding unnervingly loud in the silence of my room. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space, needing to move, needing to escape the suffocating feeling of being watched, even across fiber optic cables.

[story 3]

It wasn't just one post; it was a pattern. Every time I opened an academic forum, or even checked the school's official page, there was a subtle, almost imperceptible shift—a mention, a tag, a direct interaction from Mr. Harrison. I felt a rising heat in my cheeks, a flush that spread down my neck. When I finally navigated to his personal account, the sheer volume of his curated life felt invasive. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to physically contain the sudden surge of unpleasantness. My jaw tightened, and I could feel the tension radiating from my shoulders. The air in the room seemed thick, heavy with unspoken awkwardness, as I stared at the screen, unable to look away, the feeling of being cataloged settling deep within my core.
